story:
I taught my daughter to lie before she could spell her name. Told her people were soft, always hungry for a story that let them feel generous. Our first pitch was the truth: an amputee raising his kid alone. Soon we learned how different details hooked different hearts. The tale became a toolkit. I curated; she delivered with big eyes and borrowed grief. We didn’t call it theft. We called it survival in a world that had stopped caring.

Only a fool, I said, bets on the compassion of strangers. Back then, she only nodded. Lately, she doesn’t wait for me to speak.

We kept moving, switching towns before faces and stories wore thin. June picked the marks now. Not because I agreed, but because she was precise and proud. I used to scan crowds the way I once scanned rooftops—looking for tremors, drifts, need. June was the hook: a girl with a too-big rucksack, a tremble in her voice, sometimes a fake bruise.

“The guy in the cardigan,” she said once. “Boardwalk.”

“Why him?”

“He thinks the world still owes him something.”

“What’s the story?”

“Something tragic.” A shrug. “I’ll improvise.”

I used to polish lies. Now she wrote them on the fly, better than I ever did. She admired me, even when I couldn’t look at her without remembering the woman I lost and blaming the wrong person for it.

Her cons got sharper. She came home beaming after making a stranger cry, certain she was his niece from Pittsburgh. At night I heard her practicing voices: little-girl sweet, hollow and broken, whatever the scene required. She even sang a line meant to shatter someone’s heart. It was good. Too good.

“You scare me sometimes,” I said.

She smiled like I’d pinned a medal on her.

At a care home, she asked to try something. I idled in the lot while she tied her scarf like a school bow and found a gray man by the fish tank. I watched her mouth Grandpa. Watched him lift his head, hope rising like a tide. He cried, laughed, pressed his ring into her hand. She kissed his forehead, then slipped back into the car. “Solid gold,” she said, rolling it in her palm, laughing low. She didn’t just lie. She fed on how easy it was to move people.

Pride and rot warred in me. I’d seen this before—the young who shed their leashes, drunk on power; the ones who went feral, scanning for weakness like it was a skill. Once I hated that look. Now I saw it in my child.

“You enjoyed it?” I asked.

“Yeah,” she said. “Of course.”

I couldn’t sleep. Her lit-up face burned the ceiling. In the morning I said we were done for a while. She didn’t argue. We drove west through small towns where those who had anything kept it close. In a quiet sea place, I found bookkeeping and dispatch work at the docks and told her we’d be honest. She rolled her eyes and vanished most days. One night she returned late, knuckles scabbed. In the bathroom haze she asked, “You hate me now, don’t you?”

“No,” I said. Then the worst lie I’d told: “You did nothing wrong.”

I sat awake, and the truth finally stood up. I’d always sworn it was for her—food, clothes, safety. It was for me. For my anger, my loss. She took it in because she wanted me to see her. I never did. Not the child, not the student, not the echo of the woman I loved. I saw the damage—and the hollow I’d carved and filled with my bile. She isn’t me; she’s worse, because I made her reason.

That night I packed. Left money and the car keys. No note. I took the road on my crutches and walked away. Not because I didn’t love her. Because I finally did.
